2014.text: What is the goal of the paper?

The goal of the paper @misc{2014} is to explore the use of self-attention mechanisms in neural networks, particularly in the context of sequence-to-sequence models. The authors aim to address three key desiderata: minimizing the total computational complexity per layer, maximizing the amount of computation that can be parallelized, and facilitating the model's ability to learn to attend by relative positions. The paper presents a novel architecture that leverages multi-head attention to allow the model to jointly focus on different parts of the input representation effectively.

Self-attention, also known as intra-attention, is an attention mechanism that relates different positions of a single sequence to compute a representation of that sequence. It allows the model to focus on various parts of the input sequence simultaneously, capturing dependencies regardless of their distance in the sequence. This is particularly useful for tasks that require understanding long-distance relationships within the data.

In the context of the paper @misc{2014}, self-attention mechanisms are contrasted with recurrent and convolutional layers, highlighting their advantages in terms of computational complexity and parallelization. The authors note that self-attention can effectively learn to attend to different parts of the sequence, as evidenced by the behavior of various attention heads, which often exhibit patterns related to the syntactic and semantic structure of sentences. This mechanism has been successfully applied in a variety of tasks, including reading comprehension and abstractive summarization. 

Additionally, the paper emphasizes the use of multi-head attention to mitigate issues that arise from averaging attention-weighted positions, allowing the model to learn diverse tasks across different attention heads.
